#   • replace the title below with the bare "Benchmarking …" form
#   • comment out the `author:` and `author-tex:` blocks
#   • apply the per-paragraph BLIND-REVIEW SWAP comments throughout the body
#   • comment out the Future Work section (body content; blind cap is 8 pages)
#   • comment out the Acknowledgments section
#   • comment out Appendix O (Responsible NLP Checklist)
#
# Blind title:
#   title: "Benchmarking LLM Systems on Subsumption-Based Legal Reasoning in German Law"
title: "BenGER: Benchmarking LLM Systems on Subsumption-Based Legal Reasoning in German Law"
jupyter: benger-dataset-arr
author:
  - name: Sebastian Nagl
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    email: sebastian.nagl@tum.de
    attributes:
      corresponding: true
    note: "Core contributor"
  - name: Ann-Kristin Mayrhofer
    affiliation: Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Martin Heidebach
    affiliation: Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Aleyna Koçak
    affiliation: University of Konstanz
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Anne Zettelmeier
    affiliation: University of Saarbrücken
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Elly Breu
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Angelina Greiner
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Sofija Milijas
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Matthias Grabmair
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Senior author"
author-tex: |
  \author{
    Sebastian Nagl\textsuperscript{1}\thanks{\,Core Contributor, corresponding author. Correspondence to \texttt{sebastian.nagl@tum.de}.} \quad
    Ann-Kristin Mayrhofer\textsuperscript{2\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Martin Heidebach\textsuperscript{2\textdaggerdbl} \\
    \bfseries Aleyna Koçak\textsuperscript{3\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Anne Zettelmeier\textsuperscript{4\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Elly Breu\textsuperscript{1\textdaggerdbl} \\
    \bfseries Angelina Greiner\textsuperscript{1\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Sofija Milijas\textsuperscript{1\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Matthias Grabmair\textsuperscript{1}\thanks{\,Senior author.} \\[6pt]
    \mdseries \textsuperscript{1}Technical University of Munich (TUM) \quad
    \textsuperscript{2}Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU) \\
    \textsuperscript{3}University of Konstanz \quad
    \textsuperscript{4}University of Saarbrücken\thanks{\,Dataset contributor.}
  }
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

import json
from pathlib import Path
from collections import Counter

def load_json(path):
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return json.load(f)

def load_optional(path):
    try:
        return load_json(path)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return None

interim   = Path("data/interim")
raw       = Path("data/raw")
processed = Path("data/processed")

zjs = load_json(interim / "zjs.json")
grundprinzipien = load_json(interim / "grundprinzipien.json")
benchathon = load_json(interim / "benchathon.json")
variants = load_json(processed / "benchathon_instruction_variants.json")
benchathon_tasks = load_json(raw / "benchathon" / "Benchathon_export.json")["tasks"]

# Derived exports - produced by scripts/derive_paper_exports.py from the real
# Benchathon export + scripts/derive_inter_judge_agreement.py.
# Loaded if present so the manuscript still renders before derive has run.
systems_meta        = load_optional(processed / "systems.json")
model_evals         = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_model_evaluations.json")
human_grades        = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_human_grades.json")
automatic_metrics   = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_automatic_metrics.json")
human_judge_repeats = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_human_judge_repeats.json")
zjs_summary         = load_optional(processed / "zjs_model_summary.json")

n_zjs = len(zjs)
n_qa = len(grundprinzipien)
n_benchathon = len(benchathon)

_graded_ann_ids = {
    ev["annotation_id"]
    for t in benchathon_tasks
    for ev in t.get("evaluations", [])
    if "human:loesung" in (ev.get("field_name") or "")
    and ev.get("annotation_id") is not None
}
variant_counts = Counter(variants.get(aid) for aid in _graded_ann_ids)
n_human_only = variant_counts["no_ai"]
n_co_creation = variant_counts["ai"]
n_human_total = n_human_only + n_co_creation

# ---- Inline-expression bindings for prose: ----
agreement_stats_setup = load_optional(processed / "agreement_stats.json") or {}
r3 = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_metric_correlation", {})
r3_humans = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_metric_correlation_humans") or {}
CANONICAL_METRIC_ORDER = (
    "bleu", "rouge", "meteor", "chrf",
    "bertscore", "moverscore", "semantic_similarity",
)
METRIC_LABELS = {
    "bleu": "BLEU", "rouge": "ROUGE", "meteor": "METEOR", "chrf": "chrF",
    "bertscore": "BERTScore", "moverscore": "MoverScore",
    "semantic_similarity": "Semantic sim.",
}
r4 = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation", {})
r4_bereich = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_by_bereich", {}) or {}
r4_tost = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_vs_flagship_tost") or {}
r5_jh = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_vs_human", {})
r5_irr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_human_irr", {})
r5_irr_bal_raw = (r5_irr.get("balanced_subset") or {}).get("raw") or {}
r5_cal = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_calderon", {})
r5_dim = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_dim", {})
r5_jr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_repeats", {}) or {}
r4h = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_blind_human") or {}
r5_creator = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_creator_vs_blind") or {}
r5_llm = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_on_llm_solutions") or {}
r5_cal_llm = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_calderon_on_llm_solutions") or {}
r5_dim_llm = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_dim_on_llm_solutions") or {}
r5_irr_type = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_human_irr_by_solution_type") or {}
inter_judge = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_inter_judge_agreement.json") or {}
tier_agg = agreement_stats_setup.get("tier_aggregates") or {}
rank_corr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_rank_correlation") or {}
zjs_metric_corr = load_optional(processed / "zjs_metric_correlation.json") or {}
grundprinzipien_summary  = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_model_summary.json") or []
grundprinzipien_metric_corr = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_metric_correlation.json") or {}
grundprinzipien_tier_agg = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_tier_aggregates.json") or {}

def _zmc(metric, field="pearson_vs_judge_raw"):
    """Safe deep-get for ZJS per-generation metric correlation."""
    return (zjs_metric_corr.get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def _gmc(metric, field="pearson_vs_judge_raw"):
    """Safe deep-get for Grundprinzipien per-generation metric correlation."""
    return (grundprinzipien_metric_corr.get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def _ta(corpus, dim, key, field):
    """Safe deep-get for tier_aggregates so the prose tolerates missing keys.
    `corpus` is benchathon/zjs (sourced from agreement_stats.json) or
    grundprinzipien (sourced from the standalone tier file)."""
    if corpus == "grundprinzipien":
        return ((grundprinzipien_tier_agg.get(dim) or {}).get(key) or {}).get(field)
    return (((tier_agg.get(corpus) or {}).get(dim) or {}).get(key) or {}).get(field)

def _rc(corpus, metric, field="spearman_metric_vs_judge"):
    """Safe deep-get for rq3_rank_correlation."""
    return ((rank_corr.get(corpus) or {}).get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def fmt(x, w=2):
    return "-" if x is None else f"{x:.{w}f}"
def fmtp(x, w=2):
    return "-" if x is None else f"{x:+.{w}f}"
def fmtpct(x, w=0):
    return "-" if x is None else f"{x*100:.{w}f}%"

# Convenience handles into the alt-test payload. `bp` = blind-reviewer
# pool, `se` = single-expert reference (creator un-blind grade) keyed
# by ε. None-safe so the prose still renders before the script is run.
r5_cal_bp     = (r5_cal     or {}).get("blind_pool") or {}
r5_cal_se     = ((r5_cal    or {}).get("single_expert") or {}).get("epsilon_results") or {}
r5_cal_llm_bp = (r5_cal_llm or {}).get("blind_pool") or {}
r5_cal_llm_se = ((r5_cal_llm or {}).get("single_expert") or {}).get("epsilon_results") or {}

# Reviewer-requested supplementary judge-calibration data (Shapiro-Wilk +
# paired t-test on pool-substitution differences). Per-judge / per-pass rows.
judge_calib_path = processed / "judge_calibration.json"
judge_calib = load_optional(judge_calib_path) or []
def _calib(label_match):
    """Find the first calibration row whose label contains label_match."""
    for r in judge_calib:
        if label_match in r.get("label", ""):
            return r
    return {}

# Per-judge Calderon alt-test results (rq5_calderon_per_judge.py output).
# Keys (six judges, human & LLM picks):
#   baseline_human / baseline_llm        — primary GPT-5.4-mini
#   cfgB_opus_human / cfgB_opus_llm      — Opus-4.7
#   cfgB_gemini_human / cfgB_gemini_llm  — Gemini-3.1-Pro
#   deepseek_human / deepseek_llm        — DeepSeek-V4-Pro
#   qwen_human / qwen_llm                — Qwen3.5-397B-A17B
#   sonnet_human / sonnet_llm            — Sonnet-4.6
calderon_per_judge = load_optional(processed / "calderon_per_judge.json") or {}
def _cpj(key):
    return (calderon_per_judge.get(key) or {}).get("payload") or {}

def _pass(d):
    if not d:
        return "-"
    return "passes" if d.get("passes_alt_test") else "does not pass"


# Pre-extracted dimension values (the inline-expression form
# `r5_dim.get('x', {}).get('y')` has historically tripped Quarto's
# markdown lexer because of the empty-dict literal `{}`).
def _dim(dim_name, key):
    return ((r5_dim or {}).get(dim_name) or {}).get(key)

dim_pearson_ergebnis      = _dim("ergebnisrichtigkeit", "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_vollstaendig  = _dim("vollstaendigkeit",    "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_rechtsgr      = _dim("rechtsgrundlagen",    "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_rechtsk       = _dim("rechtskenntnis",      "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_subsum        = _dim("subsumtion",          "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_schwerpunkt   = _dim("schwerpunktsetzung",  "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_method        = _dim("methodischer_stil",   "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_gliederung    = _dim("gliederung",          "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_sprache       = _dim("sprache",             "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_formalia      = _dim("formalia",            "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_mae_ergebnis          = _dim("ergebnisrichtigkeit", "mae_dim_points")
dim_mae_formalia          = _dim("formalia",            "mae_dim_points")

# Friendly labels for the rubric dimensions, in the same casing as the prose.
_dim_label = {
    "ergebnisrichtigkeit": "Result correctness",
    "vollstaendigkeit":    "Issue identification",
    "rechtsgrundlagen":    "Legal grounding",
    "rechtskenntnis":      "Doctrinal knowledge",
    "subsumtion":          "Subsumption quality",
    "schwerpunktsetzung":  "Problem depth",
    "methodischer_stil":   "Methodological structure",
    "gliederung":          "Organization",
    "sprache":             "Terminology",
    "formalia":            "Formal correctness",
}

# Sorted dim Pearson list - drives the "highest on / lowest on" prose in RQ5.
_dim_pearson_sorted = sorted(
    (
        (label, _dim(dim, "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human"))
        for dim, label in _dim_label.items()
    ),
    key=lambda kv: (kv[1] if kv[1] is not None else float("-inf")),
    reverse=True,
)
def _dim_prose_top(n):
    items = [(lbl, v) for lbl, v in _dim_pearson_sorted[:n] if v is not None]
    return ", ".join(f"{lbl} (r={fmt(v)})" for lbl, v in items)
def _dim_prose_bottom(n):
    items = [(lbl, v) for lbl, v in _dim_pearson_sorted[-n:] if v is not None]
    return ", ".join(f"{lbl} (r={fmt(v)})" for lbl, v in items)

# Short alias used throughout the paper for each model id; full ids stay
# in `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex} (Appendix C) so the body prose can reference
# systems compactly. Keep this dict in sync with the systems_meta names.
MODEL_ALIAS = {
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":                              "Sonnet-4.6",
    "claude-opus-4-7":                                "Opus-4.7",
    "gpt-5.4":                                        "GPT-5.4",
    "gpt-5.4-mini":                                   "GPT-5.4-mini",
    "gpt-5-mini":                                     "GPT-5-mini",
    "gemini-3.1-pro-preview":                         "Gemini-3.1-Pro",
    "gemini-3-flash-preview":                         "Gemini-3-Flash",
    "gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview":                  "Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite",
    "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro":                    "DeepSeek-V4-Pro",
    "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash":                  "DeepSeek-V4-Flash",
    "Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507":             "Qwen3-235B",
    "Qwen/Qwen3.5-122B-A10B":                         "Qwen3.5-122B",
    "Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B":                           "Qwen3.6-35B",
    "meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8": "Llama-4",
    # systems.json strips provider prefixes from open-weight names; alias both forms.
    "Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507":                  "Qwen3-235B",
    "Qwen3.5-122B-A10B":                              "Qwen3.5-122B",
    "Qwen3.6-35B-A3B":                                "Qwen3.6-35B",
    "Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8":         "Llama-4",
}

def alias_for(model_id):
    """Return the short alias for a model id, falling back to the id itself."""
    return MODEL_ALIAS.get(model_id, model_id)

# Per-system mean judge raw on Benchathon (for RQ1 prose).
from statistics import mean as _mean
bench_by_sys = {}
if model_evals:
    _acc = {}
    for r in model_evals:
        if r.get("raw_score") is not None:
            _acc.setdefault(r["system"], []).append(r["raw_score"])
    bench_by_sys = {k: _mean(v) for k, v in _acc.items()}

zjs_by_sys = {}
if zjs_summary:
    for r in zjs_summary:
        zjs_by_sys[r["system"]] = r["metrics"]

grundprinzipien_by_sys = {}
for r in grundprinzipien_summary:
    grundprinzipien_by_sys[r["system"]] = r["metrics"]

def b(sys_id):
    """Mean Benchathon judge raw for a system id, or None."""
    return bench_by_sys.get(sys_id)
def z(sys_id, key="llm_judge_falloesung_raw_mean"):
    return (zjs_by_sys.get(sys_id) or {}).get(key)
def g(sys_id, key="judge_raw_mean"):
    """Per-system Grundprinzipien metric (judge_raw_mean / judge_pass_rate /
    accuracy / <metric>_mean), 0-100 for judge raw, 0-1 for accuracy."""
    return (grundprinzipien_by_sys.get(sys_id) or {}).get(key)

def placeholder_panel(figsize, title, missing_files, hint):
    import matplotlib.pyplot as _plt
    fig, ax = _plt.subplots(figsize=figsize)
    ax.axis("off")
    body = f"{title}\n\nPending export(s):\n  • " + "\n  • ".join(missing_files) + f"\n\n{hint}"
    ax.text(0.02, 0.98, body, ha="left", va="top", fontsize=7,
            family="monospace", color="#444",
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.5", fc="#fff8dc", ec="#d4a017"))
    _plt.tight_layout(pad=0.3)
    return fig, ax

# Per-condition human-judge scores on Benchathon (raws, passed). Used by the
# RQ4 fig-human-vs-ai chunk (body) and the main leaderboard chunk; hoisted
# into setup so the figure can run before the leaderboard chunk in source
# order.
_human_judge_by_variant = {"no_ai": ([], []), "ai": ([], [])}
# Only include scores from the primary GPT-5.4-mini single-pass run
# (BASELINE_JUDGE_FIELD_PREFIX = llm_judge_falloesung-mptmfvee). Other
# llm_judge_falloesung-* prefixes correspond to cross-validation judges
# (Opus, Gemini, DeepSeek, Qwen, Sonnet) or Config-A intra-judge passes;
# mixing those into the human-baseline rows would average across judges.
_PRIMARY_JUDGE_PREFIX = "llm_judge_falloesung-mptmfvee"
for _t in benchathon_tasks:
    for _ev in _t.get("evaluations") or []:
        _fn = _ev.get("field_name") or ""
        if not _fn.startswith(_PRIMARY_JUDGE_PREFIX) or "human:loesung" not in _fn:
            continue
        _m = _ev.get("metrics") or {}
        # GPT-5.4-mini run uses Shape A (nested under `llm_judge_falloesung`);
        # the legacy single-pass GPT-5-mini run used Shape B (flat keys).
        _judge = _m.get("llm_judge_falloesung")
        _details = _judge.get("details") if isinstance(_judge, dict) else None
        _rs = (_details or {}).get("raw_score") if _details else _m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw")
        _ps = (_details or {}).get("passed") if _details else _m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_passed")
        if _rs is None:
            continue
        _v = variants.get(_ev.get("annotation_id"))
        if _v not in _human_judge_by_variant:
            continue
        _human_judge_by_variant[_v][0].append(float(_rs))
        _human_judge_by_variant[_v][1].append(bool(_ps))
```

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: only the opening sentence changes for blind mode.
Replace "We introduce the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset for
evaluating LLM systems on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law.
The BenGER dataset consists of three components:" with the de-identified
form "We introduce a benchmark for evaluating LLM systems on subsumption-
based legal reasoning in German law. The dataset comprises:" - keep the
`\begin{abstract}` / `\end{abstract}` LaTeX wrappers in both modes; the
ACL formatter requires this environment, not a markdown heading. -->
```{=latex}
\begin{abstract}
```

We introduce the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset for evaluating LLM systems on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. The dataset combines `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"` exam-style free-text legal case tasks across multiple levels of legal education with `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks, and includes a controlled validation subset of timed human-written solutions under both unaided and human–AI co-creation conditions. We evaluate `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems - closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight - with a rubric-aligned LLM-as-a-Judge cross-validated against a multi-rater human-grading layer (three blind reviews per solution, six judge families benchmarked against the human pool). Closed-flagship systems lead the leaderboard across all three corpora, human–AI co-creation measurably improves on unaided human work, and the LLM judge tracks human grading at Pearson r=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('pearson_raw'),2)` and Cohen's κ=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('passfail_cohen_kappa'),2)`, with system rankings stable across judge families and two judges from independent providers clearing the Calderon single-reviewer replacement bar on human-authored solutions.
```{=latex}
\end{abstract}
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Emit the fig-overview float here, very early in the body, so that LaTeX's
# \afterpage hook fires after page 1 ships out -- placing the figure at the
# top of page 2. The matplotlib PDF itself is generated by the chunk further
# down (after the Introduction); chunk execution order is source order, but
# the PDF file is on disk before LaTeX runs either way. Keep this chunk in
# sync with the figure builder below.
#
# Because the float is emitted as raw LaTeX (not a Quarto-native figure chunk),
# Quarto's `@fig-overview` crossref does NOT resolve to it. Body prose
# references this figure as `Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} -- every other
# figure in the paper uses Quarto-native `@fig-...` crossrefs.
print(
    r"\afterpage{%" "\n"
    r"\begin{figure*}[t!]" "\n"
    r"\centering" "\n"
    r"\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{assets/fig-overview.png}" "\n"
    r"\caption{Overview of the benchmark and evaluation pipeline. "
    r"Three German-law corpora -- the published ZJS exam corpus, the short "
    r"Doctrinal Principles question set, and the controlled Benchathon "
    r"validation subset with human solutions in two working conditions -- "
    r"are answered by a panel of LLM systems and scored by a rubric-aligned "
    r"LLM judge. The Benchathon subset is additionally graded by a blind "
    r"human reviewer pool (with one author-informed creator review per "
    r"solution); the resulting human distribution is used to validate the "
    r"LLM judge.}" "\n"
    r"\label{fig-overview}" "\n"
    r"\end{figure*}%" "\n"
    r"}" "\n"
)
```

# Introduction

Evaluating legal reasoning is hard. Grading of legal exam answers exhibits substantial variability even among human experts: Hufeld [@hufeld2024] shows that identical answers in German law exams receive markedly different scores depending on the grader, reflecting the interpretive nature of doctrinal legal analysis. This poses a challenge for recent work on large language models (LLMs): if human evaluation itself is noisy, what does it mean to benchmark legal reasoning performance?

Existing benchmarks for legal NLP have evolved rapidly from classification [@chalkidis2022lexglue; @guha2023legalbench] to reasoning capabilities [@fei2024lawbench; @fan2026lexam; @jia2025readyjuristone; @shi2026plawbench] and retrieval-augmented evaluation [@pipitone2024legalbenchrag; @butler2026legalragbench], but most emphasise short-form QA, common-law jurisdictions, or stable ground-truth labels - leaving open-ended subsumption-based German legal reasoning under noisy expert judgements under-served.

We address these gaps with a benchmark centered on subsumption-based legal reasoning, the core reasoning paradigm in German law (@sec-legal-methodology).

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace this paragraph with the de-identified form:
"We present a benchmark^[Full code and dataset will be released upon paper
acceptance.] for evaluating LLMs on subsumption-based legal reasoning in
German law. The dataset comprises `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"`
exam-style free-text legal case tasks across different stages of legal
education and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks. The
benchmark also incorporates human baselines for traditional legal work and
human–AI co-creation on a controlled Benchathon validation subset.
`Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} summarises the three corpora and the
LLM-generation-plus-evaluation pipeline."
(Drop the "BenGER" name, drop the GitHub URL, drop the platform
self-citation sentence.) -->
We present the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset^[Full code and dataset are available at <https://github.com/SebastianNagl/benger-platform>.] for evaluating LLMs on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. The BenGER dataset consists of three components covering `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"` exam-style free-text legal case tasks across different stages of legal education and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks, and incorporates human baselines for traditional legal work and human–AI co-creation on a controlled Benchathon validation subset. `Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} summarises the three components and the LLM-generation-plus-evaluation pipeline. Generation, annotation, and evaluation were run end-to-end on the BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger], the open-source web application we previously developed for benchmarking legal-domain LLM systems.

To evaluate free-text legal reasoning under noisy human judgements, we adopt a hybrid framework: an LLM-as-a-Judge aligned with German grading rubrics, cross-validated against three blind human reviews per solution and an author-informed creator review as a reference signal. We treat evaluation as a distribution over plausible expert assessments rather than a single deterministic label.

Our contributions are threefold: (i) we introduce a benchmark for subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law, (ii) we provide an empirical evaluation of `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems - closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight - alongside human and co-creative baselines, and (iii) an evaluation methodology that accounts for the inherent variability of legal grading.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: drop "of the full BenGER dataset" so the sentence
reads "We organise the empirical analysis around five questions." -->
We organise the empirical analysis of the full BenGER dataset around five questions. **RQ1 (Performance):** how well do contemporary LLM systems solve open-ended subsumption-based German legal reasoning tasks, and how do they compare to human baselines? **RQ2 (System-tier effects):** how does performance vary across system tiers and open-/closed-weight access modes? **RQ3 (Evaluation validity):** how well do generic automatic metrics correlate with rubric-based legal evaluation? **RQ4 (Human–AI co-creation):** does AI-assisted drafting improve human performance, and how does it compare to standalone LLM systems? **RQ5 (Grading reliability):** how closely do LLM-judge evaluations align with the empirical distribution of human grades, and are their deviations comparable to inter-rater variability?

# Subsumption-based legal reasoning {#sec-legal-methodology}

Subsumption-based legal reasoning is the core reasoning paradigm of German civil-law doctrine [@larenz1995methodenlehre; @moellers2023methodenlehre]. Unlike common-law systems, where reasoning proceeds primarily through analogy to precedent, German legal analysis derives outcomes from codified statutory norms via a structured four-step scaffold - *Obersatz* (the legal claim or norm hypothesis to be examined), *Definition* (interpretation of the norm's elements), *Subsumtion* (anchoring of the concrete facts under those elements), and *Ergebnis* (the resulting legal consequence). This *Gutachtenstil* register (opinion style, where the conclusion follows the reasoning) - distinct from the result-first *Urteilsstil* (judgement style) used in judicial decisions - is the form in which German legal education, state examinations, and entry-level legal practice are trained and assessed; grading targets the written *Falllösung* end-to-end rather than the final answer alone, because the rubric rewards how each norm element is anchored in the specific fact pattern. This makes subsumption-based reasoning a structurally demanding generation task: a system has to identify the governing norm, articulate its elements, anchor every element in the facts, and conclude - a chain in which surface fluency can mask a missing *Subsumtion* step. It also makes the task unusually amenable to rubric-based evaluation, because each step in the scaffold maps to an identifiable component of the generated answer rather than to a global quality impression. The English-language legal NLP benchmarks centered on classification, multiple-choice, or precedent-style reasoning [@chalkidis2022lexglue; @guha2023legalbench] do not exercise this scaffold, leaving German subsumption-based legal reasoning under noisy expert judgement under-served.

# Related work

Legal NLP benchmarking has evolved from classification baselines - LexGLUE [@chalkidis2022lexglue] (seven English legal datasets) and LegalBench [@guha2023legalbench] (162 mostly short-form US-federal-law tasks) - to reasoning-centric, jurisdiction-specific evaluation. Recent work targets jurisdiction-specific reasoning: LawBench [@fei2024lawbench] and LexEval [@li2024lexeval] for Chinese civil law, KBL [@kim2024kbl] for Korean bar-exam questions, LAR-ECHR [@chlapanis2024larechr] for European Court of Human Rights argument chains, KOBLEX [@lee2025koblex] for provision-grounded multi-hop reasoning in Korean law, GreekBarBench [@chlapanis2025greekbarbench] for free-text Greek bar exams, NitiBench [@akarajaradwong2025nitibench] for Thai financial and tax law, PLawBench [@shi2026plawbench] for rubric-graded Chinese legal practice across 13 practical scenarios, and LaborBench [@hariri2025laborbench] for US state labor-law statutory simplification. A complementary line targets retrieval-augmented evaluation - LegalBench-RAG [@pipitone2024legalbenchrag] and Legal RAG Bench [@butler2026legalragbench] - and discrepancy-based auditing of LLM legal reasoning - CLAUSE [@choudhury2026clause]. The closest precedent is LEXam [@fan2026lexam] - 7,537 questions across 340 law exams in English and German, with an ensemble LLM-as-a-Judge validated against human experts - but its rubric is not the *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* scaffold central to German *Falllösung* grading (@sec-legal-methodology) and it does not couple its free-text items to the dimension-level grading criteria used in German law school and state-exam evaluation.

Evaluating free-text legal reasoning relies increasingly on LLM-as-a-Judge approaches, which raises concerns over bias, calibration, and alignment with expert judgement. Research in German legal education shows that human grading itself is substantially variable: Hufeld [@hufeld2024] reports an experiment with 15 beginner-level law exams and 23 graders, in which the same submissions received 15 or 16 independent grades each - the average range between the lowest and highest grade per answer was 6.47 points on the German 0–18 scale, and only 42% of grades fell within ±1 point of the answer-specific mean. Evaluation in legal reasoning tasks is therefore better treated as a distribution over plausible expert judgements than as a single ground truth, motivating hybrid methodologies that combine human expertise with calibrated LLM-based scoring.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "our prior BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger]
provides … workflows on which the present dataset was built." with the
non-self-attributing form "the BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger] provides
an end-to-end framework for German legal case analysis and
subsumption-based reasoning workflows.", and replace "The BenGER dataset
addresses this gap" with "Our benchmark addresses this gap". -->
Resources for German legal NLP remain limited: GerLayQA [@buttner2024gerlayqa] provides ~21,000 lay questions paired with lawyer answers but focuses on QA rather than subsumption-based reasoning, GerLeRB [@weber2025gerlerb] targets legislative retrieval, and our prior BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger] provides an end-to-end framework for German legal case analysis and subsumption-based reasoning workflows on which the present dataset was built. German legal reasoning grounded in doctrinal subsumption under statutory law remains under-served, especially for open-ended, structured analysis under noisy human judgements. The BenGER dataset addresses this gap by combining a `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"`-task exam-style corpus and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning items with a controlled Benchathon validation subset, a German doctrinal rubric, and a multi-rater cross-validation protocol.

# Dataset

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: "The BenGER dataset draws on three components" →
"Our benchmark draws on three corpora". -->
The BenGER dataset draws on three components covering the main forms of legal reasoning in German legal education: two exam-style case corpora in which a fact pattern is given and a structured *Falllösung* is expected, and one set of short doctrinal reasoning items that probes core principles without the full case-analysis scaffolding.

The ZJS corpus contributes `{python} n_zjs` publicly available exam-style cases from the *Zeitschrift für das Juristische Studium* (Journal of Legal Studies)^[<https://www.zjs-online.com/>], covering civil, criminal, and public law and spanning early undergraduate exercises through first and selected second state-examination materials, each paired with an expert-written reference solution. The Benchathon corpus adds `{python} n_benchathon` newly collected exam-style tasks at an intermediate difficulty level (more demanding than introductory exercises, less complex than full first-state-exam problems), each with `{python} n_human_total` human solutions (`{python} n_human_only` traditional, `{python} n_co_creation` human–AI co-creation).^[The corpus is named after the in-person Benchathon event at which participants produced these solutions and the validation human grading. Participants were predominantly law students at various stages of legal training, plus a smaller group of *Referendare* (post-state-exam legal trainees), recent graduates, and a small layperson cohort; full composition in Appendix `\ref{benchathon-participant-composition}`{=latex}.] The Doctrinal Principles corpus (*Grundprinzipien*) contributes `{python} n_qa` short doctrinal reasoning items (question–answer pairs with explanatory reasoning) on core principles, with recent case law where appropriate. ZJS supports at-scale automated evaluation; Benchathon supports the human baseline + judge-validation layer; Doctrinal Principles probes the same reasoning at shorter form.

The benchmark is intended for evaluation only; we do not define a train/test split, since the objective is comparative evaluation of deployed LLM systems and human baselines. Pretraining-contamination considerations across the three corpora are discussed in §Limitations.

## Benchathon task design and human solution collection

Benchathon participants solved tasks in a controlled annotation environment, with each task randomly assigned to one of two conditions: in the *traditional* condition, participants could use statutory texts, legal databases, literature, and internet research; in the *co-creation* condition, participants were additionally free to use any LLM(s) of their choosing - commercial or open-weight, in any combination - through their own accounts; we did not log which tool a given participant used. The co-creation arm therefore measures an upper bound on what human–LLM teams currently achieve with off-the-shelf assistance, not a controlled comparison against any specific system. Only the final written solution is evaluated, mirroring legal examination practice; auxiliary materials (notes, outlines, span markings) were retained for future analysis but are not used here (Appendix `\ref{full-benchathon-human-evaluation-assignment-procedure}`{=latex}). Each task had a two-hour time limit; participant expertise was measured objectively (reported legal grades and qualifications) and subjectively (self-assessed competence on a Likert scale).

# Experimental setup

## Generation

We evaluate `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems through Cloud-APIs. Per-system providers, snapshots, observed temperatures, token-budget settings, truncation counts, and the short aliases used in the body are in `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex} (Appendix C). Each task received at least one generation per system; flagship and large-context systems received additional repetitions where capacity allowed. The Benchathon subset was generated by all systems; on ZJS, all `{python} f"{len(zjs_summary) if zjs_summary else 12}"` systems produced coverage of up to `{python} f"{max((r['n_tasks'] for r in zjs_summary), default=n_zjs)}"` published exams.^[Individual questions were occasionally flagged as prohibited content by the provider's safety filter, leaving a small handful of per-system coverage gaps below the corpus total.]

All systems were prompted with the same German-language system and instruction prompts (Appendix `\ref{system-prompts-for-generation}`{=latex} + `\ref{instruction-prompts-for-generation}`{=latex}) and configured to return a single JSON object containing the full case solution (*Falllösung*), with the instruction prompt mirroring the *Gutachtenstil* scaffold (@sec-legal-methodology). Provider-recommended temperatures and token budgets were used (temperature 0.0–1.0, max output 8,000–16,000 tokens); generations malformed or shorter than 200 output tokens after a bounded retry budget were excluded. Truncation in kept generations is concentrated in the smaller open-weight systems (`Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex}).

## Black-box system evaluation
We evaluate LLM systems through provider APIs in a black-box setting, so our results measure end-to-end system behaviour rather than isolated base-model capability. We do not enable retrieval or tool-use endpoints; components we cannot disable - notably content filters and, on some products, internal routing between sub-models - operate by default. This reflects realistic deployment conditions but limits causal claims about underlying architectures.

## Automatic metrics

Per generation we compute BLEU [@papineni2002bleu], ROUGE [@lin2004rouge], METEOR [@banerjee2005meteor], chrF, BERTScore [@zhang2020bertscore], MoverScore [@zhao2019moverscore], and a Sentence-BERT cosine similarity [@reimers2019sentencebert] against the expert reference solution. Per-system means appear in the main leaderboard tables; per-generation values are correlated against the LLM-judge rubric score in RQ3.


## LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation
For rubric-based automatic evaluation, we use a specialized LLM-as-a-Judge prompt aligned with German legal grading practices. The judge receives the task, the reference solution, and the solution to be evaluated. It assigns scores across ten dimensions covering result correctness (*Ergebnisrichtigkeit*), issue identification (*Vollständigkeit*), legal grounding (*Rechtsgrundlagen*), doctrinal knowledge (*Rechtskenntnis*), subsumption quality (*Subsumtion*), problem depth (*Schwerpunktsetzung*), methodological structure (*Methodischer Stil*), organization (*Gliederung*), terminology (*Sprache*), and formal correctness (*Formalia*). Dimension scores are aggregated to a 100-point raw score and mapped to the German 0–18 grade-point scale. A generation counts as a *pass* when its grade-point score reaches *ausreichend* (≥ 4 on the 0–18 scale, corresponding to a raw score of ≥ 50/100), following the German *Staatsexamen* (state law examination) convention; this is the boolean reported in the *Pass* columns of `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex} and in the pass-rate aggregates throughout. The rubric operationalises the *Gutachtenstil* scaffold (@sec-legal-methodology): each step in the *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* schema is reflected in a dedicated dimension (issue identification, legal grounding, doctrinal knowledge, subsumption quality, methodological structure), so the per-dimension scores in @fig-rubric-dim correspond to identifiable components of the reasoning process.

The judge is blind to solution origin and receives inputs in a fixed (task, reference, candidate) order. The primary judge is GPT-5.4-mini; configuration in Appendix `\ref{llm-judge-configuration}`{=latex}, prompt in Appendix `\ref{evaluation-prompts-for-the-llm-judge}`{=latex}. Since judge-based evaluation may itself be model-dependent, we cross-validate the judge against a multi-rater human-grading layer on a Benchathon subset (design below; results in RQ5). To quantify judge stability we additionally run two complementary checks on the Benchathon subset - *within-judge stochasticity* (k=3 re-runs of GPT-5.4-mini) and *between-judge bias* (additional evaluations by Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, and Sonnet-4.6); both are reported in RQ5. Outputs conform to a predefined JSON schema; non-conforming outputs are retried and reported as evaluation failures if they cannot be parsed.

## Human evaluation {#sec-human-eval}
Human evaluation is conducted on a controlled subset of 45 Benchathon solutions: one traditional human, one human–AI co-created, and one LLM-generated solution per Benchathon task. Human submissions are selected as the median-expertise participant per legal area (*Bereich*); LLM submissions are drawn through a tier-balanced rotation across closed-flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight reference systems. Each selected solution receives three independent blind reviews and one author-informed creator review (180 reviews total). Reviewers grade against the same rubric as the LLM judge and form the IRR pool used to validate the judge; creator reviews are excluded from IRR since task authors have privileged knowledge. The selection procedure, the seven-grader roster, and the $m=5$ blind subset are described in `Appendix~\ref{full-benchathon-human-evaluation-assignment-procedure}`{=latex}. We report descriptive agreement statistics - Spearman, Pearson, MAE, Cohen's $\kappa$ [@cohen1960coefficient], ICC(2,1) and ICC(2,k) [@shrout1979intraclass; @koo2016guideline] - alongside the Calderon alt-test [@calderon2025] in RQ5. For the alt-test we follow the blind-pool procedure at $\varepsilon=0.15$ (Calderon's skilled-annotator tier) with Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR at $q=0.05$ across the $m$ blind reviewers, and report the single-expert variant against the un-blind creator grade at $\varepsilon=0.20$ (with $\varepsilon=0.15$ as a sensitivity check); the judge passes when the winning rate $\omega \geq 0.5$. The primary judge is GPT-5.4-mini; we additionally re-score the same picks with Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, and Sonnet-4.6 (per-judge alt-test in `Tables~\ref{tbl-calderon-per-annotator-human}`{=latex} and `\ref{tbl-calderon-per-annotator-llm}`{=latex}). The central question is not whether the LLM judge reproduces a single human grade, but whether its deviation is comparable to that among human graders.

# Results

Unless otherwise noted, scores in this section are the LLM judge's rubric scores, as 0–100 raw points or 0–18 German grade points. The human-grading layer covers the 45-pick validation design with one author-informed creator review and three blind reviews per pick (180 reviews total). The analysis pipeline regenerates every reported number from the data file checked in alongside the manuscript.

## RQ1: Overall performance of LLM systems and humans

The unified per-system view across the three corpora is shown in `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}; per-corpus full leaderboards with surface-metric columns are in `Appendix~\ref{full-results-table}`{=latex}. On the Benchathon subset, top-tier closed systems score in the upper 60s on the 0–100 judge scale, with Opus-4.7 (`{python} fmt(b('claude-opus-4-7'),1)`), Gemini-3.1-Pro (`{python} fmt(b('gemini-3.1-pro-preview'),1)`), and GPT-5.4 (`{python} fmt(b('gpt-5.4'),1)`) clustered at the top with pass rates above 90%, followed by Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite (`{python} fmt(b('gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview'),1)`) and Sonnet-4.6 (`{python} fmt(b('claude-sonnet-4-6'),1)`) in the upper 50s to lower 60s. The flagship open-weight systems DeepSeek-V4-Pro and Qwen3-235B trail the top closed models by roughly `{python} fmt(b('claude-opus-4-7') - b('deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro'),0)`–`{python} fmt(b('claude-opus-4-7') - b('Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507'),0)` raw points. The same ordering broadly reproduces on ZJS, where GPT-5.4 leads at `{python} fmt(z('gpt-5.4'),1)` raw points (`{python} fmt(z('gpt-5.4','grade_points_mean'),1)` grade points, `{python} fmtpct(z('gpt-5.4','pass_rate'),0)` pass) and the weakest open-weight Llama-4 trails at `{python} fmt(z('meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8'),1)` raw points (`{python} fmt(z('meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8','grade_points_mean'),1)` grade points, `{python} fmtpct(z('meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8','pass_rate'),0)` pass).

There is substantial within-task variance: per-system standard deviations across the Benchathon tasks span roughly 8–17 raw points across all tiers under the GPT-5.4-mini judge, so single-task performance is a noisy signal even for top-tier systems. On the Benchathon subset, the human cohort scores in the mid-range of the LLM distribution under both conditions, with the co-creation condition shifted upward (see human rows in `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex} and @fig-human-vs-ai in RQ4).

On the short-form Doctrinal Principles corpus the same flagship cluster leads: Gemini-3.1-Pro (`{python} fmt(g('gemini-3.1-pro-preview'),1)`), Opus-4.7 (`{python} fmt(g('claude-opus-4-7'),1)`), Sonnet-4.6 (`{python} fmt(g('claude-sonnet-4-6'),1)`), and GPT-5.4 (`{python} fmt(g('gpt-5.4'),1)`). Doctrinal Principles items carry a Ja/Nein (yes/no) decision label; across systems accuracy ranges from `{python} fmtpct(min((r['metrics'].get('accuracy') or 0) for r in grundprinzipien_summary), 0)` to `{python} fmtpct(max((r['metrics'].get('accuracy') or 0) for r in grundprinzipien_summary), 0)`, with Gemini-3.1-Pro at `{python} fmtpct(g('gemini-3.1-pro-preview','accuracy'),0)` and Opus-4.7 at `{python} fmtpct(g('claude-opus-4-7','accuracy'),0)`.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
# Table helpers (_tex_escape, emit_tex_table) and a few formatting maps used
# by both this body chunk and downstream appendix chunks. Defined here so the
# unified main leaderboard chunk below - which sits inside the Results
# section to anchor the table* float near RQ1/RQ2 - can use them without
# depending on the deferred chunks at the end of the document.
def _fmt(v):
    if v is None: return "-"
    if isinstance(v, float):
        return f"{v:.2g}" if abs(v) < 1 else f"{v:.0f}"
    return str(v)

_tier_label = {"flagship": "Flagship", "efficiency": "Efficiency",
               "open_reference": "Open ref."}
_access_label = {"provider_api": "Provider API",
                 "managed_inference": "Managed inf."}


def _tex_escape(s):
    """Minimal LaTeX escape for cells with text from the dataset."""
    if s is None:
        return "---"
    s = str(s)
    replacements = (
        ("\\", r"\textbackslash{}"),
        ("&", r"\&"), ("%", r"\%"), ("$", r"\$"), ("#", r"\#"),
        ("_", r"\_"), ("{", r"\{"), ("}", r"\}"),
        ("~", r"\textasciitilde{}"), ("^", r"\textasciicircum{}"),
        ("—", "---"), ("–", "--"),
    )
    for a, b in replacements:
        s = s.replace(a, b)
    return s


def emit_tex_table(label, caption, headers, rows, *,
                   col_spec=None, span_two_columns=False,
                   first_col_left=True, tex_escape=True,
                   tabcolsep_pt=4, fontsize_cmd=r"\footnotesize",
                   placement="t", fill_width=False, shrink_to_fit=False):
    """Print a native LaTeX float wrapping a booktabs tabular.

    fill_width=True switches the inner environment from ``tabular`` to
    ``tabular*`` with ``@{\\extracolsep{\\fill}}`` so the table stretches to
    the full text-column width of the float (\\columnwidth / \\textwidth)
    without rescaling fonts.

    shrink_to_fit=True wraps the tabular in ``\\resizebox{<width>}{!}{...}``
    so over-wide tables shrink to the float width instead of bleeding into
    the right margin. Forces a plain ``tabular`` (not ``tabular*``) because
    ``\\resizebox`` can't take an explicit-width tabular* as its argument.
    Use this for appendix tables with many numeric columns.
    """
    n_cols = len(headers)
    if col_spec is None:
        col_spec = ("l" if first_col_left else "c") + "c" * (n_cols - 1)
    env = "table*" if span_two_columns else "table"
    target_w = r"\textwidth" if span_two_columns else r"\columnwidth"
    out = [
        f"\\begin{{{env}}}[{placement}]",
        r"\centering" + fontsize_cmd,
        r"\caption{" + caption + "}",
        f"\\label{{{label}}}",
        f"\\setlength{{\\tabcolsep}}{{{tabcolsep_pt}pt}}",
    ]
    if shrink_to_fit:
        out.append(f"\\resizebox{{{target_w}}}{{!}}{{%")
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular}}{{{col_spec}}}")
    elif fill_width:
        col_spec_filled = "@{\\extracolsep{\\fill}}" + col_spec + "@{}"
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular*}}{{{target_w}}}{{{col_spec_filled}}}")
    else:
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular}}{{{col_spec}}}")
    out += [
        r"\toprule",
        " & ".join(headers) + r" \\",
        r"\midrule",
    ]
    for r in rows:
        if len(r) == 1:
            out.append(r[0] + r" \\")
            continue
        cells = [(_tex_escape(c) if tex_escape else (c if c is not None else "---"))
                 for c in r]
        out.append(" & ".join(cells) + r" \\")
    out.append(r"\bottomrule")
    if shrink_to_fit:
        out.append(r"\end{tabular}")
        out.append(r"}%  end \resizebox")
    else:
        out.append(r"\end{tabular*}" if fill_width else r"\end{tabular}")
    out.append(f"\\end{{{env}}}")
    out.append("")
    # Write the LaTeX to a per-label asset file and emit `\input{}` via a
    # raw-LaTeX block. Without this, Pandoc parses any `\begin{table*}` in
    # the chunk's `output: asis` stream as a Pandoc-managed float and its
    # caption brace counter truncates `\caption{...}` arguments that contain
    # nested math braces (e.g. `$\bar{\Delta}_{\text{dir}}$` - the inner
    # `}` is read as the close of `\caption{`). Routing through `\input{}`
    # bypasses Pandoc's float / caption processing entirely.
    from pathlib import Path as _Path
    _assets = _Path("assets")
    _assets.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    _safe = label.replace("-", "_")
    _path = _assets / f"{_safe}.tex"
    _path.write_text("\n".join(out), encoding="utf-8")
    print("```{=latex}")
    print(f"\\input{{assets/{_safe}.tex}}")
    print("```")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Unified main leaderboard across the three corpora. Sits in the Results
# section (between RQ1 and RQ2) so the table* float lands near its first
# reference. Replaces the previously separate per-corpus body tables; the
# surface-metric-rich per-corpus leaderboards live in Appendix~E. The two
# Benchathon human-baseline rows are pinned at the bottom.

import math, statistics
from collections import defaultdict

# Raw cells in the table are rendered as "mean ± half-CI" where half-CI is the
# half-width of a 95% interval on the per-system mean. Two derivations:
#   - Benchathon: percentile bootstrap CI from per-generation raw scores in
#     model_evals (scipy.stats.bootstrap).
#   - ZJS / Grundprinzipien: t-based CI from the per-system stdev added to the
#     summary JSONs by derive_zjs_summary.py and derive_grundprinzipien_summary.py
#     (Welford's online algorithm).

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats as _sp_stats

_BENCH_BOOT_RNG = np.random.default_rng(7)

def _bench_boot_ci(raws, n_boot=2000):
    """Half-width of a 95% percentile-bootstrap CI on the mean."""
    if len(raws) < 3:
        return None
    res = _sp_stats.bootstrap(
        (np.asarray(raws, dtype=float),), np.mean,
        n_resamples=n_boot, method="percentile",
        random_state=_BENCH_BOOT_RNG,
    )
    return (res.confidence_interval.high - res.confidence_interval.low) / 2.0

def _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n):
    """Half-width of a 95% Gaussian CI on the mean (1.96 · SE)."""
    if mean is None or stdev is None or n is None or n <= 1:
        return None
    lo, hi = _sp_stats.norm.interval(0.95, loc=mean, scale=stdev / math.sqrt(n))
    return (hi - lo) / 2.0

_bench_by_sys_full = defaultdict(list)
for r in (model_evals or []):
    _bench_by_sys_full[r["system"]].append(r)

def _bench_agg(sys_id):
    rs = _bench_by_sys_full.get(sys_id) or []
    raws = [x["raw_score"] for x in rs if x.get("raw_score") is not None]
    ps   = [bool(x["passed"]) for x in rs if x.get("passed") is not None]
    return (len(raws) if raws else None,
            statistics.mean(raws) if raws else None,
            _bench_boot_ci(raws),
            (sum(ps) / len(ps)) if ps else None)

_zjs_by_sys_full = {r["system"]: r for r in (zjs_summary or [])}
def _zjs_agg(sys_id):
    r = _zjs_by_sys_full.get(sys_id)
    if not r: return (None, None, None, None)
    m = r.get("metrics") or {}
    mean = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_mean")
    stdev = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_stdev")
    n_w = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_n") or r.get("n_generations")
    return (r.get("n_generations"), mean,
            _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n_w),
            m.get("pass_rate"))

_gp_by_sys_full = {r["system"]: r for r in (grundprinzipien_summary or [])}
def _gp_agg(sys_id):
    r = _gp_by_sys_full.get(sys_id)
    if not r: return (None, None, None, None, None)
    m = r.get("metrics") or {}
    mean = m.get("judge_raw_mean")
    stdev = m.get("judge_raw_stdev")
    n_w = m.get("judge_raw_n") or r.get("n_generations")
    return (r.get("n_generations"), mean,
            _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n_w),
            m.get("judge_pass_rate"),
            m.get("accuracy"))

_all_sys_ids = set(_bench_by_sys_full) | set(_zjs_by_sys_full) | set(_gp_by_sys_full)

def _mean_avail(sys_id):
    vals = []
    for fn in (_bench_agg, _zjs_agg, _gp_agg):
        v = fn(sys_id)
        if v[1] is not None: vals.append(v[1])
    return statistics.mean(vals) if vals else 0.0

_sys_order = sorted(_all_sys_ids, key=lambda s: -_mean_avail(s))

def _raw_ci(mean, half):
    """Compact 'mean ± half' cell; falls back to '---' if mean missing."""
    if mean is None:
        return "---"
    if half is None:
        return f"{mean:.1f}"
    return f"{mean:.1f}\\,$\\pm$\\,{half:.1f}"

def _pct(v):  return "---" if v is None else f"{100*v:.0f}\\%"
def _n(v):    return "---" if v is None else str(v)

_rows = []
for sys_id in _sys_order:
    bn, br, br_ci, bp = _bench_agg(sys_id)
    zn, zr, zr_ci, zp = _zjs_agg(sys_id)
    gn, gr_, gr_ci, gp_r, ga = _gp_agg(sys_id)
    _rows.append([
        _tex_escape(alias_for(sys_id)),
        _n(bn), _raw_ci(br, br_ci), _pct(bp),
        _n(zn), _raw_ci(zr, zr_ci), _pct(zp),
        _n(gn), _raw_ci(gr_, gr_ci), _pct(gp_r), _pct(ga),
    ])

def _human_row(label, raws, passed):
    if not raws:
        return [label, "0", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---"]
    return [label, str(len(raws)),
            _raw_ci(statistics.mean(raws), _bench_boot_ci(raws)),
            f"{100*sum(passed)/len(passed):.0f}\\%",
            "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---"]

_human_all_raw  = _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0] + _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0]
_human_all_pass = _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][1] + _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][1]

_lines = [
    r"\begin{table*}[t]",
    r"\centering\footnotesize",
    "\\caption{Main leaderboard across the three corpora. Per-system mean "
    "LLM-judge raw score (0--100, mean\\,$\\pm$\\,half-width of a 95\\% CI: "
    "percentile bootstrap $B=2000$ on Benchathon for small per-system $n$, "
    "analytic 1.96\\,$\\cdot$\\,SE on ZJS and Doctrinal Principles) and pass "
    "rate; Doctrinal Principles also reports Ja/Nein (yes/no) decision accuracy. "
    "Systems are ranked by the mean of their available per-corpus raw scores. "
    "The two human-baseline rows use the LLM judge's grades of human-written "
    "Benchathon solutions.}",
    r"\label{tbl-main-leaderboard}",
    r"\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}",
    r"\begin{tabular*}{\textwidth}{@{\extracolsep{\fill}} l rrr rrr rrrr @{}}",
    r"\toprule",
    r" & \multicolumn{3}{c}{Benchathon}"
    r" & \multicolumn{3}{c}{ZJS}"
    r" & \multicolumn{4}{c}{Doctrinal Principles} \\",
    r"\cmidrule(lr){2-4} \cmidrule(lr){5-7} \cmidrule(lr){8-11}",
    r"System / Group & $n$ & Raw & Pass"
    r" & $n$ & Raw & Pass"
    r" & $n$ & Raw & Pass & Acc. \\",
    r"\midrule",
]
for row in _rows:
    _lines.append(" & ".join(row) + r" \\")

_lines.append(r"\midrule")
for label, raws, passed in [
    ("Human (all)",       _human_all_raw, _human_all_pass),
    (r"\quad traditional", _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0], _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][1]),
    (r"\quad co-creation", _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0],    _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][1]),
]:
    _lines.append(" & ".join(_human_row(label, raws, passed)) + r" \\")

_lines += [r"\bottomrule", r"\end{tabular*}", r"\end{table*}", ""]
print("\n".join(_lines))
```

## RQ2: Scaling and system effects

Closed flagship systems from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google occupy the top of every per-corpus leaderboard, while efficiency-tier closed systems and the strongest open-weight systems (DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3-235B) form an overlapping mid-band - closed-API dominance narrows substantially below the flagship tier. Aggregating by tier, the flagship-vs-open-weight gap is `{python} f"{(_ta('benchathon','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('benchathon','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` raw points on Benchathon and narrows to `{python} f"{(_ta('zjs','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('zjs','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` on ZJS; the closed-vs-open weights gap shrinks similarly from `{python} f"{(_ta('benchathon','by_weights','closed','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('benchathon','by_weights','open','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` to `{python} f"{(_ta('zjs','by_weights','closed','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('zjs','by_weights','open','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` raw points. The same direction holds on Doctrinal Principles, so the flagship-vs-open-weight ordering survives the three different task formats and the 4-dimension rubric variant. We frame these as system-level rather than model-level effects, since black-box API access entangles the open–closed split with provider-side prompts, decoding, post-processing, and possible retrieval or tool use (see Limitations).

## RQ3: Evaluation validity - automatic metric correlation

Across all three corpora, lexical surface metrics correlate with the LLM judge's rubric raw score more strongly than embedding-based metrics, running partly against the common expectation that semantic-embedding metrics dominate lexical ones on long-form legal text. On Benchathon, METEOR (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('meteor',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), ROUGE (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('rouge',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), and BLEU (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('bleu',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`) lead while MoverScore (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('moverscore',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), BERTScore (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('bertscore',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), and sentence-embedding similarity (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('semantic_similarity',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`) trail; the same ordering broadly holds on ZJS and Doctrinal Principles (`Appendix~\ref{automatic-metric-vs.-llm-judge-correlation}`{=latex}: `Table~\ref{tbl-metric-correlation}`{=latex}, @fig-metric-correlation). This is consistent with the highly templated structure of doctrinal German exam writing, where reference and high-quality system answers share substantial n-gram material around statutory citations and the *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* scaffolding. At the per-system level on Benchathon, the lexical metrics' Spearman against the judge ranking reaches +`{python} fmt(_rc('benchathon','meteor'))` (METEOR) while BERTScore (+`{python} fmt(_rc('benchathon','bertscore'))`) and semantic similarity (+`{python} fmt(_rc('benchathon','semantic_similarity'))`) trail - a leaderboard built on a strong lexical metric would broadly preserve the judge's coarse-tier ordering but disagree on the mid-tier, motivating a calibrated LLM judge as the primary scoring instrument.

## RQ4: Human–AI co-creation under LLM-judge scoring

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-human-vs-ai
#| fig-cap: "Mean LLM-judge raw score (0–100) per group on the Benchathon validation subset, with 95% bootstrap CIs on each group's mean. The two AI bars pool per-generation scores across all evaluated LLM systems (*pooled*) and across the closed-flagship tier only (*flagship*)."
#| fig-pos: 't'

# Single-column figure (Quarto-native crossref via @fig-human-vs-ai). Compact
# four-group bar chart sized for a single column of the ACL two-column
# layout (~3.3 in wide). The flagship-only AI bar makes the co-creation ≈
# closed-flagship-tier comparison visually immediate.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats as _sp_stats

_HVSA_BOOT_RNG = np.random.default_rng(11)

def _hvsa_boot_ci(xs, n=2000):
    arr = np.asarray([float(x) for x in xs if x is not None], dtype=float)
    if arr.size < 3:
        return (float(arr.mean()) if arr.size else 0.0, 0.0, 0.0)
    res = _sp_stats.bootstrap(
        (arr,), np.mean, n_resamples=n, method="percentile",
        random_state=_HVSA_BOOT_RNG,
    )
    return (float(arr.mean()),
            float(res.confidence_interval.low),
            float(res.confidence_interval.high))

_flagship_ids = {s["model_id"] for s in (systems_meta or []) if s.get("tier") == "flagship"}
_flagship_raws = [r["raw_score"] for r in (model_evals or [])
                  if r.get("raw_score") is not None and r.get("system") in _flagship_ids]

_groups = [
    ("Human\ntraditional", _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0]),
    ("Human\nco-creation", _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0]),
]
if model_evals:
    _ai_raws = [r["raw_score"] for r in model_evals if r["raw_score"] is not None]
    _groups.append(("AI all\n(pooled)", _ai_raws))
if _flagship_raws:
    _groups.append(("AI flagship\n(closed)", _flagship_raws))

_labels = [g[0] for g in _groups]
_points, _los, _his = zip(*(_hvsa_boot_ci(g[1]) for g in _groups))
_ns = [len(g[1]) for g in _groups]

_fig, _ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(2.95, 1.9))
_xs = range(len(_groups))
_ax.bar(_xs, _points,
        yerr=[[p - l for p, l in zip(_points, _los)],
              [h - p for p, h in zip(_points, _his)]],
        color=["#6c8eb0", "#b08056", "#a89bc8", "#6f5fa0"][:len(_groups)],
        capsize=2.5, edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.4, width=0.55)
for _x, _p, _n in zip(_xs, _points, _ns):
    _ax.text(_x, _p + 1.5, f"{_p:.1f}", ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=6.5, weight="bold")
    _ax.text(_x, 2, f"$n$={_n}", ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=5.8, color="#444")
_ax.set_xticks(list(_xs))
_ax.set_xticklabels(_labels, fontsize=6.5)
_ax.set_ylabel("LLM-judge raw (0–100)", fontsize=6.5)
_ax.tick_params(axis="y", labelsize=6)
_ax.set_ylim(0, 100)
_ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
_ax.yaxis.grid(True, linestyle=":", linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.6)
_ax.set_axisbelow(True)
plt.tight_layout(pad=0.2)
plt.show()
```

Co-creation solutions score higher than traditional ones on the Benchathon - by `{python} fmtp(r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad'),1)` raw points on average, about `{python} fmtp((r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)*18/100,1)` grade points on the 0–18 scale (@fig-human-vs-ai; participant-clustered 95% CI [`{python} fmtp(r4.get('ci95_lo'),1)`, `{python} fmtp(r4.get('ci95_hi'),1)`]). The gap holds within the `{python} f"{r4.get('n_participants_paired')}"` participants who completed both arms, repeats across all three legal domains, and is in fact a lower bound on the per-participant gain because less-experienced participants skipped traditional items more often than co-creation ones - leaving the traditional pool expertise-biased upward (full robustness arc in `Appendix~\ref{rq4-supplementary}`{=latex}). At that level the AI-assisted humans sit alongside the best standalone LLMs we evaluated: a TOST equivalence test rules out any difference of more than $\pm 5$ raw points against the closed-flagship tier (`Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}). The one substantive caveat is on magnitude - when the same solutions are re-graded by blind human reviewers on the RQ5 validation subset, the gap shrinks to about `{python} f"{int(abs((r4h.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)))}"` raw points, `{python} fmtp(abs((r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0) - (r4h.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)),0)` points smaller than the LLM-judge estimate, a small absolute discrepancy consistent with the GPT-5.4-mini judge's near-zero content-dependent calibration offset and within the blind-pool's inter-rater spread (Limitations).

## RQ5: Grading reliability

The reliability question for the leaderboard is whether the LLM judge can stand in for a blind human reviewer. We answer it in three stages: how closely the judge agrees with humans on a per-solution basis, whether it clears the formal substitution bar, and what its disagreements look like when they happen.


Across the validation subset, the primary GPT-5.4-mini judge agrees with the per-solution mean of the blind reviewers at Pearson r=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('pearson_raw'))`, pass/fail Cohen's $\kappa$=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('passfail_cohen_kappa'))`, and MAE `{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_raw'),1)` raw / `{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_grade_points'),1)` grade points, with a per-solution offset of just `{python} fmtp(r5_jh.get('judge_minus_pool_mean_raw'),1)` raw points (so the bias-corrected MAE is essentially unchanged at `{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_raw_bias_corrected'),1)` / `{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_grade_points_bias_corrected'),1)`). The reviewers themselves disagree by an average within-annotation max--min spread of `{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_raw'),1)` raw points - comparable to the several-grade-point range Hufeld [@hufeld2024] documents for German legal grading - and the pool reaches ICC(2,$k$)=`{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('icc_2_k_raw'))`, so the judge sits inside the cloud of human disagreement rather than outside it (full agreement panel - Spearman, by-provenance ICCs, balanced rater-triplet for the partially-crossed design - in `Table~\ref{tbl-agreement}`{=latex}).


Per-solution agreement does not by itself establish that the judge can *replace* a randomly drawn reviewer. The Calderon alt-test sets a stricter bar at $\varepsilon=0.15$ with Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR (`§\ref{sec-human-eval}`{=latex}); we apply it across six judge families - the primary GPT-5.4-mini, plus Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, and Sonnet-4.6. Two judges clear the $\omega \geq 0.5$ replacement bar on the human-authored picks: GPT-5.4-mini at $\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('baseline_human').get('winning_rate'),2)` and Opus-4.7 at $\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('cfgB_opus_human').get('winning_rate'),2)`. The two judges from independent providers reject the same blind reviewers as the ones they can statistically replace, despite originating from completely different model families - a convergence that argues against same-provider judge bias as a meaningful confound. Sonnet-4.6 ($\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('sonnet_human').get('winning_rate'),2)`) and Gemini-3.1-Pro ($\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('cfgB_gemini_human').get('winning_rate'),2)`) are one annotator-rejection short of the bar; DeepSeek-V4-Pro and Qwen3.5-397B-A17B fail at $\omega \leq 0.20$. The expert-reference variant against the un-blind creator additionally passes for the primary GPT-5.4-mini judge on human picks ($\omega=$ `{python} fmt((r5_cal_se.get('0.20') or {}).get('winning_rate'),2)` at $\varepsilon=0.20$) but fails on LLM picks ($\omega=$ `{python} fmt((r5_cal_llm_se.get('0.20') or {}).get('winning_rate'),2)`) (`Table~\ref{tbl-agreement}`{=latex}; per-judge $\omega$, per-annotator $p$-values, and a stacked-bootstrap robustness check in `Appendix~\ref{sec-judge-calibration-supplementary-detail}`{=latex}). The judges that fail the replacement bar cluster among those whose per-solution offset against the human pool is largest - a calibration gradient (`Appendix~\ref{sec-judge-calibration-supplementary-detail}`{=latex}).


The disagreement is also patterned by rubric dimension (@fig-rubric-dim): agreement is highest on dimensions with structured scoring criteria and lowest on presentation and form. Several outcome-and-reasoning dimensions drop on LLM-generated solutions - Result correctness from r=`{python} fmt(dim_pearson_ergebnis)` (human-authored) to r=`{python} fmt((r5_dim_llm.get('ergebnisrichtigkeit') or {}).get('pearson_judge_vs_mean_human'))` (LLM), with comparable drops on Issue identification, Doctrinal knowledge, and Problem depth - consistent with judge–human divergence on outcomes reached via unfamiliar reasoning paths.


Re-running GPT-5.4-mini $k=3$ times on the Benchathon LLM generations yields a within-cell stdev of `{python} fmt(r5_jr.get('mean_within_gen_stdev'),2)` raw points, substantially tighter than the inter-human within-solution spread of `{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_raw'),1)` raw points - so the calibration offset is not noise the judge could re-roll out of. Across the six judge families on the `{python} f"{inter_judge.get('n_cells_with_all_judges')}"`-generation subset, the strictness gradient (Opus-4.7 strictest, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B most lenient; `Table~\ref{tbl-judge-calibration}`{=latex}) leaves pairwise rank agreement at Spearman $\rho$ between `{python} fmt(min((p.get('spearman_rho') or 1) for p in (inter_judge.get('pairwise') or {}).values()),2)` and `{python} fmt(max((p.get('spearman_rho') or 0) for p in (inter_judge.get('pairwise') or {}).values()),2)` across all fifteen pairs, and $\omega$ drops, not rises, from human to LLM picks for every judge - arguing against same-family inflation.

## Error analysis

A qualitative inspection of the LLM-generated solutions surfaces five recurring failure modes - patterns the rubric flags but a single-number metric would miss. The most common is *correct outcome, weak subsumption* - 9 instances, concentrated in the open-weight mid-tier, where the doctrinally expected conclusion is reached via abstract argumentation rather than fact-bound *Subsumtion*. A related length-bound pair surfaces around *Subsumtion* itself: *missing or perfunctory subsumption in short answers* in 16 generations below 200 words where the answer labels rather than subsumes, and a complementary *long-but-shallow* pattern in two generations exceeding 2,200 words with weak Subsumption quality and Problem depth, where lexical metrics over-reward what the rubric does not. Finally, *wrong-norm framing* - low Legal grounding and Doctrinal knowledge together, indicating an unsuitable doctrinal frame rather than misexecution within the correct one - is documented qualitatively without a global count. The cleanest pointer at judge-failure cases is a fifth pattern, *wrong outcome despite plausible reasoning*: 5 instances where the judge praises fact-bound *Subsumtion* ($\geq 0.60\cdot\text{max}$) but the final answer is wrong. A judge that conflated reasoning and outcome would be visible here as systematic praise of reasoning preceding a wrong result, so the rubric's separation of those two dimensions is doing real work. Patterns are identified by high within-rubric dimension dispersion combined with at least one dimension below $0.40\cdot\text{max}$, against a denominator of `{python} f"{len(model_evals) if model_evals else 0}"` LLM-system generations, and are reported descriptively without multiple-comparison correction; representative task identifiers, dimension scores, and judge-justification quotes are available in the released analysis pipeline.

# Discussion

Capability is concentrated in the closed-flagship tier, but the margin is jurisdiction- and length-sensitive. Across the three corpora the top of the leaderboard is consistently occupied by Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, and GPT-5.4, with Sonnet-4.6 joining on Doctrinal Principles. The flagship-vs-open-weight gap is ~`{python} f"{(_ta('benchathon','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('benchathon','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` raw points on Benchathon, narrows to ~`{python} f"{(_ta('zjs','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('zjs','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` on ZJS, and shrinks further on Doctrinal Principles. The strongest open-weight systems (DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3-235B) overlap the efficiency-tier closed band on the longer corpora and reach within a few points of the flagships on the short one, so the practical question for German legal NLP is not whether open-weight systems can be used, but whether the residual flagship advantage is paid for by the cost and access constraints in `Appendix~\ref{operational-metadata}`{=latex}.


Lexical surface metrics beat embedding metrics, but neither replaces a rubric judge. Across all three corpora BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR correlate with the rubric judge more strongly than BERTScore, MoverScore, or sentence-similarity (METEOR r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('meteor',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))` vs.\ BERTScore r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('bertscore',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))` on Benchathon, reproducing on ZJS and Doctrinal Principles; `Appendix~\ref{automatic-metric-vs.-llm-judge-correlation}`{=latex}). This runs against the expectation that semantic-embedding metrics dominate lexical ones on long-form text, and is most parsimoniously explained by the templated structure of doctrinal German exam writing - high-quality answers share substantial n-gram material around statutory citations and the *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* scaffold. A lexical-only leaderboard would preserve the coarse tier ordering but disagree on the mid-tier, so the rubric judge remains the primary instrument and surface metrics read as cheap correlates.


Human–AI co-creation lifts unaided humans into the closed-flagship band, with a magnitude caveat. AI-assisted human work scores `{python} fmtp(r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad'),1)` raw points (≈ `{python} fmtp((r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)*18/100,1)` German grade points) above unaided human work under the judge, a gap that holds within the `{python} f"{r4.get('n_participants_paired')}"` participants who completed both arms and across the three legal areas; a TOST equivalence test rules out any difference of more than $\pm 5$ raw points against the closed-flagship LLM tier (`Appendix~\ref{rq4-supplementary}`{=latex}). Blind-human re-grading on the RQ5 subset shrinks the gap to `{python} fmtp(r4h.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad'),1)` raw points, narrowing the headline by ~`{python} f"{int(abs((r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0) - (r4h.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad') or 0)))}"` points; direction and qualitative conclusion are unchanged, and the residual sits within blind-pool inter-rater spread.


Two judges from independent providers clear the substitution bar. The primary GPT-5.4-mini judge tracks the per-solution blind-reviewer mean at Pearson r=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('pearson_raw'),2)` and pass/fail $\kappa$=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('passfail_cohen_kappa'),2)` and sits inside the human disagreement cloud (ICC(2,$k$)=`{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('icc_2_k_raw'),2)`). On the Calderon alt-test at $\varepsilon=0.15$, GPT-5.4-mini and Opus-4.7 both clear the $\omega \geq 0.5$ substitution bar on human-authored picks ($\omega=$ `{python} fmt(_cpj('baseline_human').get('winning_rate'),2)` and `{python} fmt(_cpj('cfgB_opus_human').get('winning_rate'),2)`) and reject the same blind reviewers; the other four (Sonnet-4.6, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B) fall short along a content-dependent leniency gradient documented in `Appendix~\ref{sec-judge-calibration-supplementary-detail}`{=latex}. Inter-judge Spearman across the six families stays between `{python} fmt(min((p.get('spearman_rho') or 1) for p in (inter_judge.get('pairwise') or {}).values()),2)` and `{python} fmt(max((p.get('spearman_rho') or 0) for p in (inter_judge.get('pairwise') or {}).values()),2)`, so the RQ1–RQ2 ordering survives any single judge's absolute-score bias. The convergence of two judges from independent providers - on both the bar and the specific reviewers they replace - argues against same-provider bias as a meaningful confound; an Opus-anchored cross-check of the primary scores is a natural absolute-calibration follow-up. Where the judge still diverges, the disagreement concentrates on presentation and on outcomes reached via unfamiliar reasoning paths (highest agreement on Result correctness r=`{python} fmt(dim_pearson_ergebnis,2)` and Issue identification r=`{python} fmt(dim_pearson_vollstaendig,2)`; lowest on Formal correctness r=`{python} fmt(dim_pearson_formalia,2)` and Terminology r=`{python} fmt(dim_pearson_sprache,2)`; Result-correctness Pearson drops to r=`{python} fmt((r5_dim_llm.get('ergebnisrichtigkeit') or {}).get('pearson_judge_vs_mean_human'),2)` on LLM-generated solutions) - supporting LLM judges as scalable raters alongside humans rather than standalone ground truth, with judge-model choice treated as a deliberate calibration knob.

# Conclusion

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "the BenGER dataset" with "a benchmark";
drop ", all run end-to-end on our previously released BenGER platform
[@nagl2026benger]"; and replace the closing release sentence with
"The benchmark will be released as an open evaluation framework with
corpora, judge prompts, rubric, and analysis pipeline upon acceptance."
(no project name, no self-citation, no GitHub URL) -->
We introduce the BenGER dataset for subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law, combining two exam-style corpora (ZJS, Benchathon) with a short doctrinal-reasoning corpus (Doctrinal Principles), a Benchathon validation subset with human and human–AI baselines, a rubric-aligned LLM-as-a-Judge, and a multi-rater cross-validation protocol - `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` systems and `{python} f"{sum((s.get('repetitions') or 0) for s in (systems_meta or [])) + (sum(r['n_generations'] for r in zjs_summary) if zjs_summary else 0) + (sum(r['n_generations'] for r in grundprinzipien_summary) if grundprinzipien_summary else 0)}"` generations on Benchathon, ZJS, and Doctrinal Principles, all run end-to-end on our previously released BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger]. BenGER is released as an open evaluation framework with corpora, judge prompts, rubric, and analysis pipeline at <https://github.com/SebastianNagl/benger-platform>.

# Limitations {.unnumbered}

Several limitations bound our findings:

All LLM systems are evaluated through hosted provider APIs in a black-box setting, so reported scores reflect end-to-end deployed behaviour, not isolated base-model capability. We do not vary provider-side prompts, decoding configurations, retrieval, or tool use, and so avoid claims about the contribution of individual architectural choices.

The dimension rubric and the LLM-judge prompt were co-developed for this benchmark rather than adopted from independent prior work; we validate them as a pair against the multi-rater human-grading layer (RQ5), whose within-annotation inter-rater spread (`{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_grade_points'),1)` grade points) sits within the range Hufeld [@hufeld2024] documents for German legal grading. Neither component has been validated in isolation.

The Benchathon subset comprises `{python} f"{n_benchathon}"` tasks and a modest participant pool. This suffices for the per-task, per-system comparisons and the judge validation reported here, but limits statistical power for fine-grained interaction effects such as expertise-stratified co-creation analyses.

Judge bias is a known confounder in LLM evaluation [@zheng2023mtbench; @panickssery2024self]; we address it via the multi-rater agreement protocol on the Benchathon validation subset (RQ5) and the inter-judge agreement analysis across six families (GPT-5.4-mini, Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, Sonnet-4.6), which shows a clear strictness gradient but pairwise rank agreement comparable to inter-human agreement. <!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "in BenGER" with "in our benchmark". -->
Same-provider preference is a specific structural risk in BenGER because the primary judge GPT-5.4-mini shares a model family with leaderboard-leading GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.4-mini systems, and Sonnet-4.6 (Benchathon cross-validation judge) shares its family with leaderboard-leading Opus-4.7 and Sonnet-4.6 systems.
